questions.text: You are developing a TensorFlow model that analyzes multilingual news content to track economic signals for a global research firm. The model takes many hours to train on each update cycle, and your training pipeline includes automatic checkpointing every five minutes. Your team wants to reduce infrastructure costs as much as possible while keeping training performance reasonable. What setup should you use?

options.text: Run a custom training job in Vertex AI using a non-preemptible v5e-8 TPU
options.explanation:
The best choice is the preemptible v5e-8 TPU. Preemptible TPUs offer substantial cost savings and are ideal when training can resume from checkpoints, as in this case. v5e TPUs also offer better performance and efficiency compared to older TPU versions.

Non-preemptible TPUs are more reliable but significantly more expensive.

A100 GPUs are powerful but overkill for this use case if cost is the main concern.

T4 GPUs are affordable but may be too slow for large-scale training.

questions.text:
You are working as a machine learning engineer for a streaming service. The product team wants to improve the recommendation engine so that users are shown new shows or movies based on their watch history and preferences of users with similar behavior. You are asked to design a model to support this initiative.

What type of model should you build?

options.text: Rule-based system
options.explanation:
Collaborative filtering is well suited for recommending items based on past user behavior and similarities across users. It does not require detailed metadata about the content itself. Instead, it focuses on patterns in what users have interacted with and what similar users have liked or watched.

A regression model could predict ratings, but it would rely heavily on labeled data and content features, and it would not fully capture user-to-user similarity. A knowledge-based filtering model helps when recommendations are based on explicit content relationships, not patterns in user behavior. Rule-based systems rely on heuristics or popularity but do not personalize results based on individual watch history.

questions.text: You work for a logistics company that tracks thousands of delivery vehicles in real time. Your team has trained a TensorFlow model that predicts the likelihood of a delivery being late based on current GPS data, driver history, and weather conditions. Incoming location data is sent to Pub/Sub, and a Dataflow streaming job processes these messages, writes predictions to BigQuery, and sends alerts for high-risk deliveries. You need to integrate your model into the pipeline while keeping end-to-end latency as low as possible for immediate alerting. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the model to a Vertex AI prediction endpoint and make requests from the Dataflow pipeline
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to integrate the trained TensorFlow model directly into the Dataflow job so that predictions are executed locally in the same process as the pipeline steps. This avoids network round trips to external services and keeps latency to a minimum, which is crucial when working with real-time streams that drive immediate actions such as alerting dispatch teams. Since the model is preloaded as a dependency, the pipeline can handle large volumes of messages with consistent and predictable inference times.

The other options all introduce external calls that add latency and potential points of failure. Calling a Vertex AI endpoint or a TensorFlow Serving instance on GKE means each prediction involves network overhead, which can be unpredictable under load. Using Cloud Run for inference also requires an HTTP call and could be subject to cold starts, further delaying results. These approaches are more suitable when you need to decouple model hosting from the processing pipeline, but they do not align with the strict low-latency requirement described in this scenario.

questions.text: You are working for an analytics startup that uses scikit-learn to train regression models for forecasting customer demand. The training process on your current local workstation is too slow, and you want to migrate the training job to Vertex AI Training to speed it up without rewriting the code. What should you do first?

options.text: Run the training job in Vertex AI Training using GPUs
options.explanation:
The correct answer is to train the scikit-learn model in Vertex AI Training using CPUs with more cores and memory. Scikit-learn does not provide native GPU acceleration, so moving the job to GPUs would not improve training time. Using CPUs in Vertex AI Training allows you to choose machine types with more cores and higher memory, which can significantly reduce training time without changing the existing code. This approach avoids the complexity of converting to a different framework or modifying the training logic for distributed execution.

The other options are not the best first step for this situation. Training on GPUs will not help because scikit-learn cannot leverage GPU hardware directly. Switching to TensorFlow could allow GPU use but would require substantial refactoring before you could even test performance improvements. Running the job on multiple Compute Engine VMs in parallel would require building a distributed training setup that scikit-learn does not natively support, adding unnecessary overhead for this problem.

questions.text: A healthcare analytics company deploys machine learning models on Vertex AI to predict patient readmission risk. Hospital clients have requested per-prediction transparency that shows which features most influenced each result. Some models were built with AutoML and others are custom-trained. You need one consistent approach that integrates with your existing deployment on Vertex AI and requires minimal ongoing maintenance. What should you do?

options.text: Export prediction results to BigQuery and run a daily job to calculate feature importance scores for each model
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to enable Vertex Explainable AI for the deployed models. This service supports both AutoML and custom models and integrates directly into the existing prediction flow. When enabled, it produces feature-based explanations with each prediction, allowing clients to see which inputs contributed most to a particular result. This approach avoids building and maintaining separate infrastructure for explainability and meets the requirement for consistent, low-maintenance integration across multiple model types.

The other options have limitations. Running a SHAP explainer on Cloud Run Functions would require significant custom engineering, version control, and operational upkeep. Calculating feature importance in BigQuery as a daily batch process would only provide aggregate-level insights, not per-prediction detail. Vertex AI Model Monitoring focuses on detecting data drift and skew, not generating explanations for individual predictions, so it does not meet the stated requirements.

questions.text: A media streaming company has built a TensorFlow ranking model to recommend shows to users. The training data comes from a BigQuery query that performs complex feature engineering, including aggregations and instance-level transformations. The company needs a weekly retraining pipeline to update the model, which will serve daily personalized recommendations. They want to keep preprocessing logic consistent between training and inference while reducing long-term maintenance overhead. How should they design the pipeline?

options.text: Create the pipeline using the TensorFlow Extended SDK, use the ExampleGen component with the BigQuery executor to ingest data, implement transformations with the Transform component, and use the Trainer component to launch a Vertex AI training job
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to build the pipeline with TensorFlow Extended, using the ExampleGen component to ingest from BigQuery and the Transform component to encode the preprocessing steps. This approach solves the training-serving skew problem by ensuring that the same transformations are applied at both training and serving. The Trainer component can then launch the Vertex AI training job with the transformed features, maintaining alignment between preprocessing and model execution over time.

The other options fail to address training-serving skew effectively. Running the preprocessing SQL separately with Kubeflow Pipelines keeps the logic outside the model graph, which can cause discrepancies at inference time. Rewriting the preprocessing in Python for Dataflow adds development overhead and still does not guarantee transformation consistency. Embedding preprocessing directly in the input function is inefficient and makes the pipeline harder to maintain, while also failing to resolve skew issues in a robust way.

options.text: Create the pipeline using the TensorFlow Extended SDK, run preprocessing inside the model’s input function, use the ExampleGen component to read data from BigQuery, and then train the model with the Trainer component

questions.text: Your team is developing a fraud detection system for a global payment processing company. You are using TensorFlow to train a binary classifier on 80 billion transaction records stored in multiple CSV files. During training, your data pipeline is becoming a bottleneck and slowing down your experiments. You need to increase data throughput during training. What should you do?

options.text: Load the data into Cloud Bigtable and connect your model pipeline to read from it
options.explanation:
TFRecord format is designed to support efficient binary encoding and fast sequential reading, which makes it suitable for large-scale TensorFlow training jobs. Using TFRecords avoids the overhead of CSV parsing and integrates smoothly with tf.data pipelines. Storing the shards in Cloud Storage provides a scalable and highly available backend that supports parallel access across distributed training jobs.

BigQuery is built for analytics and SQL-style querying rather than high-performance training input. Cloud Bigtable is optimized for low-latency point reads and does not offer the throughput or compatibility needed for model training pipelines. HDFS is not native to Google Cloud and adds unnecessary infrastructure and maintenance overhead.

questions.text: A financial trading firm built hundreds of ML models to detect fraudulent transactions in real time. Each trading server runs continuously and processes thousands of events per second. The models are retrained daily and must be updated without downtime. Predictions must be generated with extremely low latency, well under a millisecond, and the deployment should be cost-efficient while running 24/7. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy a Dataflow batch pipeline and call a Vertex AI Prediction endpoint for inference
options.explanation:
A Dataflow streaming pipeline with the RunInference API is built for ultra-low-latency, high-throughput inference scenarios like fraud detection. It can process streaming data continuously and supports sub-millisecond predictions. With automatic model refresh, the firm can update all models daily without stopping or redeploying the pipeline, ensuring uninterrupted service. This approach meets both the latency and uptime requirements while keeping operational costs under control.

The other options either introduce higher latency or fail to meet the real-time requirement. Batch pipelines are not suited for continuous event processing and would add unacceptable delays. Vertex AI Prediction endpoints, even with autoscaling, have higher inference latency than RunInference and cannot guarantee sub-millisecond performance. Manually redeploying models each day is operationally inefficient and risks downtime.

You are training a convolutional neural network for satellite image classification using a Cloud TPU. You notice that the TPU utilization is low, and training time is significantly longer than expected. Profiling the training job reveals that after each training step, the TPU is idle while waiting for data to be read, decoded, and normalized from your Cloud Storage bucket. CPU usage is consistently high, and there is a delay between the end of one training step and the start of the next.

What should you do to reduce training time in a cost-efficient way?

options.text: Switch to a newer version of Cloud TPU and increase the batch size
options.explanation:
The trace indicates that the TPU is waiting for data, which suggests an input pipeline bottleneck. To reduce idle time and improve performance without increasing costs, you should optimize the data input pipeline by adding parallel reads, parallel transformations, and prefetching. This allows the next batch to be prepared while the current one is being processed by the TPU.

Upgrading to a newer TPU version or switching to GPUs would raise costs and still leave the data pipeline as a bottleneck. questions.text: You are helping a logistics company build a model to forecast delivery delays using historical package data. You achieve a training AUC of 98% within your first few experiments, despite using a simple logistic regression model and minimal feature engineering. You haven't yet tuned the model or introduced any regularization. What should you do next to validate your results?

options.text: Use k-fold cross-validation to verify the model’s consistency across different data segments
options.explanation:
When a simple model reaches near-perfect performance right away, it's often a sign that the model has access to leaked information. Data leakage can occur when a feature contains data that wouldn’t realistically be available at prediction time or is too closely tied to the label. In this case, some feature might be capturing downstream or proxy information, such as a late scan timestamp or customer complaint that correlates directly with delay outcomes.

Rather than jumping into tuning or model simplification, your first step should be to review the data pipeline and feature set. Removing features that are highly correlated with the target reduces leakage risk and gives you a better sense of the model’s true generalization performance. Techniques like cross-validation can help assess stability but won't fix the root issue if leakage exists. Likewise, hyperparameter tuning won't solve the fundamental problem of invalid input data.

questions.text:
A financial services company is building a voice-based assistant that helps customers with their account inquiries. Voice transcriptions will be stored for quality assurance and analytics. Before storing this data, the company must ensure that any sensitive customer details, such as account numbers or addresses, are removed or replaced so they cannot be used to identify individuals.

What should you do?

options.text: Use the Cloud Natural Language API to extract entities from the transcriptions and store them in a separate table for secure access
options.explanation:
The DLP API is designed to detect and transform sensitive information in text, images, and other data sources. In this case, it can scan the transcribed conversations, identify sensitive elements like account numbers, and then mask or tokenize them so that no identifiable information is stored. This approach ensures compliance with privacy requirements and reduces the risk of exposing sensitive customer data while still allowing the processed text to be used for analysis.

The other options have limitations that make them less appropriate here. Using Speech-to-Text with simple phrase filtering would be less reliable because it does not match the breadth or accuracy of the DLP API’s pattern detection. Entity extraction from Cloud Natural Language is not intended for PII detection or anonymization, so storing those entities separately would still risk leaving sensitive data in the original transcripts. Encrypting all customer data via DLP before storage is not the right use of the API in this context, as the goal is to remove or transform sensitive fields rather than encrypt the entire dataset, which would make it unusable for further analysis without decryption.

questions.text: A retail company uses Vertex AI Pipelines to train and deploy forecasting models that predict product demand. The pipeline reads historical sales data from BigQuery, exports it to Cloud Storage as TFRecord files, trains the model in Vertex AI Training, and deploys the resulting model to a Vertex AI Endpoint. One of the deployed models is producing unusually high forecast errors. You have identified the exact model version responsible and need to retrieve the precise dataset that was used for its training. How should you locate that dataset?

options.text: Retrieve the Vertex AI Training job ID associated with the model version and examine its logs to find references to the TFRecord files in Cloud Storage
options.explanation:
Vertex AI Metadata automatically records lineage between pipeline steps, which includes connections between a trained model and the data preprocessing step that produced its training dataset. By querying the metadata for the identified model version, you can trace directly to the step that exported the TFRecord files to Cloud Storage and retrieve the dataset location without guesswork. This approach ensures accurate recovery of the training data with built-in tooling designed for artifact tracking in ML workflows.

The other options are less reliable. Searching in Cloud Logging depends on log retention and may miss older pipeline runs. Manually storing datasets in BigQuery could work only if the process was set up in advance, which is not indicated here, and it bypasses the native tracking Vertex AI already provides. Pulling information from Vertex AI Training job logs can reveal file paths but lacks the structured artifact relationships that metadata lineage provides, making it harder to ensure you have the exact dataset used in training.

What should you do to maintain the relevance of your test dataset?

options.text: Add new book cover images to your existing test dataset as they are used in retraining
options.explanation:

questions.text: You manage an ML-powered product recommendation system for a large e-commerce retailer. Every week, you collect all user interaction data sent to your model’s prediction API. To measure accuracy, you send a subset of the most recent requests to a third-party human review service. After operating for a year, you see that model accuracy sometimes drops quickly, while other times it remains stable for several months. The human review service is expensive, but you also want to avoid prolonged periods of degraded recommendations. You need to determine an efficient process for deciding when to trigger the human review process and potentially retrain the model. What should you do?

options.text: Use historical accuracy trends to create a fixed review schedule for the coming year, regardless of detected changes in input data
options.explanation:
The correct answer is to build a periodic data drift detection job that compares recent serving data against the training dataset and sends data for review only when drift is detected. This method monitors for changes in feature distributions that could signal a future drop in model accuracy. By triggering reviews only when drift is found, you avoid the cost of constant reviews while still responding promptly to shifts that may lead to performance degradation. It is a proactive approach that adapts to real changes in the data rather than relying on rigid schedules or purely cost-driven rules.

The other options fall short for different reasons. Training a binary classifier on the original dataset to detect outliers will not necessarily capture the kinds of distribution shifts that lead to performance issues, so it may miss important changes. Using historical trends to set a fixed schedule ignores the unpredictable nature of real-world data changes, which can occur at any time. Basing review frequency solely on a cost-to-revenue comparison is too simplistic and reactive, as it does not provide an early warning mechanism to prevent accuracy drops before they impact the business.

questions.text: You are building a recommendation system for a video streaming service. Your training data includes user profile fields such as ZIP code, device ID, and content preferences. Some of these categorical features have tens of thousands of unique values. You plan to train a deep neural network. How should you encode these high-cardinality features to prepare them as model inputs?

options.text: Map the categorical values into hash buckets with a fixed bucket size
options.explanation:
When working with categorical features that have very large cardinality, hashing is a memory-efficient way to represent the data without full one-hot encoding. Hash buckets allow you to map any string value into a smaller, fixed-size space using a hash function. This avoids creating massive input vectors while still preserving enough uniqueness for learning useful patterns in neural networks.

Label encoding with direct IDs can introduce unintended ordinal relationships. PCA is inappropriate on one-hot encoded categorical data because it assumes numeric relationships. Word embeddings are typically designed for text sequences, not isolated categorical fields like ZIP codes or device IDs.

questions.text: A travel company operates a website where customers can search for vacation packages. The current search tool only matches keywords in package titles and descriptions. Recently, more users have started entering conversational queries that include specific attributes like destination, travel dates, and activities. You need to quickly build a proof-of-concept search tool that can interpret these types of queries and return relevant packages without requiring you to train and deploy your own model. How should you build this search tool?

options.text: Create the search tool with Vertex AI Agent Builder
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use Vertex AI Agent Builder. This service enables rapid creation of applications that can understand and respond to complex, natural language queries over structured datasets. It does not require you to train your own model or implement backend retrieval logic, which makes it well-suited for quickly delivering a proof-of-concept under time constraints. It can integrate with your structured vacation package data and interpret conversational queries that include specific attributes like location or activities.

The other options all require more setup and ongoing management. Vertex AI Vector Search would need you to build or source an embedding model, create and maintain a vector index, and implement custom retrieval logic. Hosting a BERT-based model on a Vertex AI endpoint demands fine-tuning, deployment work, and ongoing model management. Using a foundation model from Model Garden would require building a prompt-handling and retrieval backend along with possible tuning for your dataset, adding unnecessary complexity compared to a purpose-built tool for structured data search.

questions.text:
You work for a subscription-based streaming service and need to develop a BigQuery ML model that predicts the number of new subscribers for each region each month. Your dataset in BigQuery includes columns such as signup date, region, subscription plan, and promotional campaign ID. You want to choose the combination of model type and feature engineering approach that will best capture important patterns in the data while keeping the workflow straightforward.

What should you do?

options.text: Use a deep neural network model. Create embedding vectors for categorical features and transform dates into sequential day counts
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to use linear regression in BigQuery ML with one-hot encoding for categorical variables and derived features from the date column. One-hot encoding preserves the categorical nature of the variables without introducing false ordinal relationships. Extracting elements like month and day of week provides the model with cyclical and seasonal patterns that often influence subscriber counts. This combination is simple to implement in BigQuery ML and aligns with the goal of capturing meaningful patterns without overcomplicating the workflow.

The other options have clear drawbacks for this task. K-means clustering is for unsupervised learning and would not generate predictions for a numeric target like subscriber counts. A deep neural network with embeddings can handle categorical data but introduces unnecessary complexity and tuning requirements for this scale of problem. ARIMA is intended for time series forecasting on a single metric over time, not for incorporating multiple categorical predictors in a regression context. Label encoding in both the ARIMA and k-means options risks imposing artificial order on categories, which can mislead these models.

questions.text: You are deploying a trained image classification model on GKE using TensorFlow Serving. The model is running in a production environment on CPU-only nodes and is receiving thousands of prediction requests every second. The current response times are too slow, but you cannot change the infrastructure configuration, add GPUs, or redesign the model architecture. What should you do to improve latency?

options.text: Switch to a version of TensorFlow Serving that includes universal binary support
options.explanation: To improve latency without modifying infrastructure or switching to GPU-based nodes, your best option is to recompile TensorFlow Serving with CPU-specific optimizations, such as AVX2 or AVX-512, and pair that with an appropriate CPU platform selection in GKE. This lets the model inference code take full advantage of the available hardware. Simply changing batch or timeout parameters does not address the fundamental processing inefficiencies on CPU. Using a universal binary version of TensorFlow Serving avoids compatibility issues but does not optimize for performance on a specific CPU type.

questions.text: An insurance company runs multiple ML workflows in Vertex AI to predict claim fraud. The workflows process data from several Google Cloud projects. You need to coordinate the training and deployment steps across projects, keep a record of each model’s lineage, and compare performance between different model versions to decide which should be promoted. Which set of Google Cloud services should be part of your workflow?

options.text: Vertex AI Feature Store, Vertex AI Experiments, and Dataproc
options.explanation:
Vertex AI Pipelines can orchestrate ML workflows across multiple projects, ensuring training and deployment steps are coordinated in a repeatable way. Vertex AI Experiments can track performance metrics across model versions, making it easier to evaluate which version should move forward. Vertex AI Metadata provides lineage tracking so you can trace each model back to its data and preprocessing steps, supporting reproducibility and clear audit trails across your workflows.

The other options either omit key services or include tools that are not well-suited for this need. Dataplex focuses on governance rather than model version tracking. TensorBoard is useful for monitoring training progress but not for comparing multiple model versions after training. Model Monitoring helps in production drift detection but does not track lineage or manage workflow orchestration. Feature Store ensures consistency in serving features but does not handle orchestrating workflows or tracking model version performance. Dataproc is for distributed data processing, not for managing ML pipelines or experiment tracking.

questions.text: You work for an online gaming company that needs to monitor player activity such as movements, in-game purchases, and session durations. These events are generated in real time from thousands of client devices. Your team uses BigQuery to analyze this data continuously for gameplay insights and fraud detection. What should you do to stream this user event data into BigQuery with low latency?

options.text: Configure Pub/Sub with a Dataflow streaming pipeline to process and write events into BigQuery
options.explanation:
The best option is to use Pub/Sub to collect streaming events and Dataflow to transform and load them into BigQuery. This architecture is designed for scalable, real-time ingestion and supports preprocessing, schema enforcement, and error handling. Pub/Sub to Dataflow to BigQuery is a very common sequence that you should be familiar with.

Sending Pub/Sub messages to a Cloud Run function can work but is more complex to scale and maintain, and still requires custom code to handle BigQuery writes.

Streaming from Cloud Storage is not real time, since Cloud Storage doesn't emit events by itself in a structured stream.

Exporting from Firestore is batch-based and better suited for historical analysis, not live event pipelines.

questions.text: A logistics company is building a model to predict package delivery delays. One of the most informative features is a categorical field that records the delivery partner, but this field is sometimes missing due to inconsistent data collection from certain regions. What should the team do to maintain model performance while handling the missing values?

options.text: Remove the delivery partner feature from the dataset if more than 10% of entries are missing
options.explanation:
The correct approach retains the feature while allowing the model to capture potential patterns in missingness. Adding a new category for missing values prevents the model from misinterpreting those rows as belonging to an existing delivery partner. The binary indicator lets the model learn whether the absence of this data itself holds predictive value. This is especially useful if the missing values are not random but tend to occur in contexts that affect delivery times, such as specific regions or vendors.

Dropping the feature discards useful information and reduces model capacity. Mode imputation makes an assumption that the missing entries resemble the most common value, which could introduce noise or bias. Filling based on past deliveries may introduce leakage or non-generalizable behavior, especially if recent partner data correlates with the target in unintended ways. Including both a placeholder category and a missingness flag gives the model the flexibility to learn the correct patterns without making unverifiable assumptions.

questions.text: Your company’s research group currently submits training jobs through a homegrown web portal backed by a pool of Compute Engine VMs. Different teams use TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, and custom CUDA code. Keeping images up to date and scaling clusters has become a burden. Leadership wants a managed approach that still lets teams package any framework they need. What should you do?

options.text: Build an Airflow environment in Cloud Composer to spin up and tear down training VMs per job
options.explanation: Vertex AI Custom Training lets teams provide a container that includes their preferred framework and dependencies, which removes the need to maintain a shared VM or cluster image. The service provisions and scales the underlying compute, handles logging and artifacts, and integrates with Vertex AI Pipelines if you later automate workflows. This matches the requirement for a managed approach while preserving flexibility across frameworks. Kubeflow on GKE can support multiple frameworks, but you would be responsible for cluster upgrades, autoscaling, and operator maintenance. Cloud Composer can orchestrate jobs, yet you would still manage images, quotas, and lifecycle of the training machines. Cloud Run Functions can front job requests, but it does not provide a training service, so you would still build and manage the execution layer yourself. Choosing Custom Training reduces operational overhead and creates a consistent submission path for every team while keeping framework choice open. It also simplifies cost control and job tracking through a single managed surface in Vertex AI.

The service provisions and scales the underlying compute, handles logging and artifacts, and integrates with Vertex AI Pipelines if you later automate workflows. This matches the requirement for a managed approach while preserving flexibility across frameworks. Kubeflow on GKE can support multiple frameworks, but you would be responsible for cluster upgrades, autoscaling, and operator maintenance. Cloud Composer can orchestrate jobs, yet you would still manage images, quotas, and lifecycle of the training machines. Cloud Run Functions can front job requests, but it does not provide a training service, so you would still build and manage the execution layer yourself. Choosing Custom Training reduces operational overhead and creates a consistent submission path for every team while keeping framework choice open. It also simplifies cost control and job tracking through a single managed surface in Vertex AI.

questions.text: You work for an international social media company that offers comment moderation for posts written in dozens of languages. To simplify operations, all comments are first translated into English using the Cloud Translation API before being processed by a classification model. options.explanation:

questions.text: A telehealth company developed a machine learning model that predicts whether a patient’s reported symptoms indicate an urgent medical condition. After a virtual consultation ends, the prediction result must be sent instantly to a triage team so they can respond without delay. The model has been tested and is ready for deployment. options.text: Run batch predictions by importing the model into Vertex AI and using the Vertex Batch Prediction service to process daily files of consultation results
options.explanation:
Deploying the model to a Vertex AI endpoint allows it to serve low-latency predictions on demand without the overhead of loading the model for each request. This is important when immediate action is needed after each consultation, as the endpoint can handle requests in milliseconds and integrates well with downstream systems for triggering alerts or workflows. It is a managed service, so scaling, reliability, and performance are handled without requiring the team to build a serving infrastructure from scratch.

The other approaches do not meet the real-time requirement. Batch prediction in Vertex AI processes large sets of inputs at scheduled intervals, which is not suitable when predictions are needed instantly after each consultation. questions.text: A biotech startup packaged a custom ML model into a Docker container for serving and deployed it to Vertex AI Model Registry. When you run an online prediction request, it fails with the error: "Model server did not start. Please confirm your model file and container settings are valid." No additional messages appear in the Vertex AI logs. You need to quickly identify the cause of the failure. What should you do?

options.text: Modify the container to expose the HTTP server on port 8080 and redeploy it to Vertex AI
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to pull the Docker image locally and run it to inspect its logs. This allows you to reproduce the environment outside Vertex AI and access detailed container startup errors that may not appear in managed service logs. Local testing can reveal issues such as missing dependencies, incorrect file paths, or other misconfigurations before redeploying to Vertex AI. It is the fastest way to pinpoint problems that prevent the model server from starting.

The other options focus on making changes without first identifying the root cause. Adding verbose logging requires another deployment cycle and still assumes the container starts successfully. Changing the HTTP port or health check path may resolve some configuration mismatches, but without the actual error details, these are speculative fixes that could miss the real issue. Debugging locally before making changes is more reliable and avoids unnecessary redeployment cycles.

questions.text: You’re building a product search ranking model for a large retailer. The model uses an embedding for query terms and is trained in TensorFlow. You plan to run Vertex AI hyperparameter tuning with the default Bayesian algorithm to choose both the embedding dimension and the learning rate. You define the embedding dimension as an INTEGER between 32 and 128, and the learning rate as a DOUBLE between 1e-05 and 1e-02. Accuracy is the only goal, and total tuning time is not a concern. How should you configure scaling for these hyperparameters and the amount of parallelism?

options.text: Use UNIT_LINEAR_SCALE for both hyperparameters and allow many parallel trials
options.explanation:
The correct approach is linear scaling for the embedding dimension, log scaling for the learning rate, and a small number of parallel trials. Embedding size is a discrete capacity choice whose impact typically changes roughly linearly across the defined range, so linear scaling gives sensible steps for exploration. Learning rate effects are multiplicative and highly sensitive, so searching it on a logarithmic scale helps the tuner probe across orders of magnitude efficiently. Bayesian optimization benefits from sequential learning between trials, so limiting parallel trials lets each new trial leverage information from previous results to pick better candidates, which tends to improve final accuracy when time is not a constraint.

The other configurations miss one or more of these points. Using log scale for the embedding dimension creates awkward jumps among integers and does not reflect how model capacity usually scales. Linear scaling for the learning rate wastes trials in unhelpful regions and can skip critical orders of magnitude where good solutions live. Allowing many parallel trials weakens the Bayesian strategy because suggestions cannot incorporate feedback from earlier runs, which turns the search into something closer to random sampling and can reduce tuning quality. Mixing the scales in the opposite way produces inefficient exploration of both parameters and is unlikely to reach the best setting within a reasonable trial budget.

questions.text:
You work for a telecom provider that uses a regression model to forecast customer call volume at each service center. The model was trained and validated with accurate historical data, but several months after deployment, it begins to produce inaccurate predictions. You discover that the patterns in the incoming data no longer match the patterns in the training set.

What should you do to address this issue?

options.text: Add batch normalization layers and retrain the model weekly
options.explanation:
Changes in input patterns over time can lead to data skew, which causes a model trained on past data to perform poorly in production. The most effective approach is to monitor for skew between the training and serving data. Once skew is detected, retraining the model on updated data ensures it stays aligned with current trends.

Hyperparameter tuning and feature pruning are useful in some cases, but they do not address the root issue when the data distribution has shifted. Batch normalization affects training dynamics but does not solve problems caused by distribution drift between training and prediction.

questions.text: You are building a model in AutoML to detect fake product reviews for an online marketplace. The model is trained on historical user reviews labeled as either genuine or fake. After initial training, you find that the model is missing a significant number of fake reviews. Reducing these misses is more important than avoiding false positives. You decide to adjust the training parameters in AutoML to improve detection. What should you do? (Choose two.)

options.text: Increase the decision threshold
options.explanation:
Lowering the decision threshold makes the model classify more cases as fake, which increases sensitivity and reduces the chance of missing fake reviews. Adding more positive examples, meaning more known fake reviews, gives the model additional patterns to learn from. This is especially valuable when the dataset is imbalanced and the event of interest (fake reviews) is rare, because the model can learn the signals that are harder to detect. Together, these changes increase the likelihood of catching more fake reviews in production.

Increasing the decision threshold would have the opposite effect and cause the model to miss even more fake reviews. Adding more negative examples does not help with detecting rare positive cases. Shortening the training time limit restricts the model’s ability to learn complex relationships in the data and would not help with the issue of missed detections.

questions.text: A retail chain wants to improve its supply chain planning by predicting daily product demand for each store. The team has historical sales data with one row per day for the last 18 months. The dataset includes total units sold, the date, and the number of promotional campaigns running that day. They want to use this information to forecast daily demand several weeks ahead and ensure the model accounts for both time-based patterns and the impact of promotions. They also want to develop and test the model as quickly as possible. What should you do?

options.text: Create a Vertex AI tabular dataset and train a forecasting model in AutoML, with units sold as the target, promotions as a covariate, and date as the time variable
options.explanation:
The correct choice is to train a forecasting model in AutoML because it can handle time series data with both a time column and known covariates. In this case, the date column serves as the time variable, and the number of promotions is included as a covariate. This setup allows the model to learn seasonal patterns, long-term trends, and the effect of promotions on demand. It is also a fast way to move from dataset to forecast without building the model manually.

The other options do not fit the problem requirements as well. BigQuery ML’s ARIMA model only handles univariate forecasting, which means it would ignore the promotions data entirely. BigQuery ML’s linear regression treats this as a standard tabular problem without modeling temporal dependencies, which weakens forecast accuracy. Vertex AI AutoML Regression also ignores the sequential nature of the data, so it would not capture time-dependent patterns properly.

questions.text: You trained a scikit-learn recommendation model for a streaming service and plan to deploy it on Vertex AI for both real-time and scheduled batch predictions. The model requires preprocessing of user interaction data before generating recommendations. You want to package the preprocessing together with the model to limit the amount of additional deployment code you need to maintain. What should you do?

options.text: Build a fully custom container for the scikit-learn model, upload it to the Vertex AI Model Registry, deploy to Vertex AI Endpoints, and run batch predictions with an instanceConfig setting to transform input data
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to use a custom prediction routine (CPR) to package both the model and its preprocessing logic, then build and upload the container image to Vertex AI. CPR allows preprocessing steps to be included within the predict() method while still leveraging the standard scikit-learn serving image. This keeps deployment simpler than building a fully custom container, while still ensuring that both online and batch predictions process the data consistently. By creating the container from the local CPR model and pushing it to the Vertex AI Model Registry, you can deploy to Endpoints for online use and also schedule batch prediction jobs from the same artifact.

The other options either rely on unsupported settings or require unnecessary complexity. The instanceConfig setting mentioned does not exist for Vertex AI batch predictions, so any approach using it would fail. A fully custom container is only necessary if CPR cannot handle the preprocessing needs, so using it here adds extra code and maintenance work without providing an advantage. Choosing a prebuilt container without CPR would not allow preprocessing logic to be built into the model artifact, making it unsuitable for this requirement.

questions.text:
You trained a regression model in Vertex AI that estimates annual customer spending for a retail company. During post-training evaluation on multiple data slices, you notice that the model consistently underestimates spending for customers older than 65. You want to address this age-related bias while maintaining similar performance on the rest of the dataset.

What should you do?

options.text: Update the model’s reference baselines to include the minimum and maximum observed age values in the data
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to examine how feature values are distributed across bucketed age groups and then fix the imbalance in the training data. If customers older than 65 are underrepresented or have systematically different feature ranges, the model can learn patterns that generalize poorly to them. By quantifying these differences per age bucket and applying targeted preprocessing such as stratified resampling, reweighting, imputation for missing values, capping outliers, or age-aware bucketing, you move the training distribution closer to what you expect at inference. This addresses the cause of the age bias and usually preserves overall offline performance because you are not discarding useful signal or forcing the model to ignore informative features

The other options do not solve the underlying problem. Dropping features that correlate with age can remove important predictors and reduce accuracy, and correlation with age does not imply those features are inappropriate to use. Changing explainability baselines only affects how attribution tools present importance and has no effect on the model’s predictions. Forcing a calibration step that matches prediction distributions above and below a threshold treats the symptom and can introduce new bias or distort well calibrated scores for other groups. These alternatives either hide the bias or damage model signal rather than correcting the feature distribution imbalance that led to the issue

By quantifying these differences per age bucket and applying targeted preprocessing such as stratified resampling, reweighting, imputation for missing values, capping outliers, or age-aware bucketing, you move the training distribution closer to what you expect at inference. This addresses the cause of the age bias and usually preserves overall offline performance because you are not discarding useful signal or forcing the model to ignore informative features

The other options do not solve the underlying problem. Dropping features that correlate with age can remove important predictors and reduce accuracy, and correlation with age does not imply those features are inappropriate to use. Changing explainability baselines only affects how attribution tools present importance and has no effect on the model’s predictions. Forcing a calibration step that matches prediction distributions above and below a threshold treats the symptom and can introduce new bias or distort well calibrated scores for other groups. These alternatives either hide the bias or damage model signal rather than correcting the feature distribution imbalance that led to the issue

questions.text: You are working on a machine learning project for a global e-commerce company to predict product return likelihood. How should you read the training data into TensorFlow for this project?

options.text: Load the data into a Pandas dataframe using the BigQuery client library, then convert it into a tf.data.Dataset
options.explanation:
The correct answer is to use TensorFlow I/O’s BigQuery Reader to stream the data directly into TensorFlow. This method connects BigQuery to your training job without extra export steps, meaning you avoid storing intermediate files and eliminate format conversion delays. For datasets of this size, the direct reader can handle high throughput efficiently while scaling to the demands of your Vertex AI training jobs. This minimizes potential ingestion bottlenecks and allows the training process to work with live data from BigQuery without first moving it into another system.

The other options all introduce extra steps that can slow down training and complicate the workflow. Loading the full dataset into a Pandas dataframe is memory-bound and will not scale for hundreds of millions of records. Exporting to CSV in Cloud Storage and reading with TextLineDataset adds unnecessary file management and parsing overhead. Converting the table to TFRecords first can improve performance in some workflows, but in this case it adds an extra processing phase and storage cost without eliminating the export bottleneck. None of these approaches match the scalability and simplicity of directly reading from BigQuery into TensorFlow.

questions.text: A retail company has built a product recommendation model using data that was cleaned and enriched in a scheduled Dataflow batch pipeline. They now plan to serve real-time recommendations to customers through a Vertex AI endpoint. To ensure that the same preprocessing steps are consistently applied in both training and serving, what should they do?

options.text: Extract the preprocessing code from the batch pipeline so it can run independently, and integrate it directly into the serving endpoint
options.explanation:
The correct approach is to extract the transformation logic from the batch pipeline so that it can be reused in the endpoint. This ensures that the exact same preprocessing steps are applied to incoming requests as were used during model training, preventing training-serving skew. Integrating this shared code into the serving layer maintains consistency without introducing unnecessary dependencies or manual duplication of preprocessing logic.

The other options fail to fully address the consistency problem. A streaming Dataflow pipeline adds latency and operational complexity while still not guaranteeing the same transformation environment. Giving preprocessing code to developers for client-side execution creates risk of divergence over time and places extra responsibility on application teams. Schema checks may detect mismatches but do not apply the required transformations, leaving preprocessing gaps unaddressed.

options.text: In a Vertex AI pipeline with a custom training job, run a single hyperparameter tuning job with Bayesian optimization targeting precision, and compare results afterward
options.explanation:

You’ve developed a model that predicts whether users will miss their next scheduled workout based on patterns in app activity and location data. How should you serve your predictions and send notifications to users?

options.text: Use Firebase Cloud Messaging to deliver notifications. Register each user by ID in Firebase, and send an alert when the system-wide average likelihood of skipped workouts crosses a threshold
options.explanation:

questions.text: You manage a Vertex AI Model Monitoring job for a recommendation model that runs in production for a streaming service. Recently, you received repeated alerts for training-serving skew. You retrained the model using an updated dataset with more recent viewing behavior and deployed it to the Vertex AI endpoint. Despite this, the skew alerts continue to appear. You want the monitoring job to reflect the changes in the retraining data so that it reports only genuine skew going forward. What should you do?

options.text: Adjust the monitoring job to check predictions less frequently to reduce the number of alerts
options.explanation:
The correct answer is to update the model monitoring job so that it uses the newer training dataset as its baseline. Vertex AI Model Monitoring compares live prediction inputs to a baseline dataset to detect drift or skew. If the baseline remains as the older dataset, the monitoring system will keep flagging skew even if the deployed model was trained on fresher data. By updating the baseline, you align the monitoring system with the current state of the model and avoid false skew alerts related to outdated reference data.

The other options do not address the root cause. Reducing the monitoring frequency might lower the alert volume but does not resolve the mismatch between the baseline and the model’s current data. Disabling the job allows the mismatch to persist and risks missing valid drift events during that period. Retraining again without updating the baseline repeats the same problem because the monitoring job would still be comparing against outdated data.
